title: Linux kernel development tools advance with Sashiko AI review, b4 integration, and API specification framework
summary: On March 17, 2026, Roman Gushchin announced Sashiko, an AI-powered code-review system that automatically reviews patches on Linux kernel mailing lists, while b4, a patch-management tool, integrated a TUI-based review workflow with AI assistance, and a new framework for specifying and verifying kernel APIs was proposed. These developments represent significant innovations in tooling for the Linux kernel community. These tools address long-standing challenges in kernel development by automating code review and improving API documentation, potentially reducing bugs and enhancing collaboration among thousands of contributors. They reflect a broader trend toward integrating AI and better tooling in large-scale open-source projects to handle complexity and scale. Sashiko uses Gemini 3.1 Pro and a multi-stage review protocol to find bugs, with initial tests detecting 53% of issues in a sample set, all missed by human reviewers, but it relies on proprietary LLMs and external funding. The b4 review tool offers a terminal-based interface with optional AI integration, while the API framework aims to create machine-readable specifications for kernel interfaces.
context: The Linux kernel is a large open-source project developed by a global community, relying on mailing lists for patch submission and review, which can be inefficient at scale. b4 is a tool created by Konstantin Ryabitsev to manage patches from mailing lists, while API specifications define how software components interact, and recent AI advancements have enabled automated code analysis.
